definition: Delusion or confusion that obscures what is actually happening and supports unwholesome patterns.
notes: The project distinguishes moha from avijjā. Delusion works best here because it names the muddled distortion operating in immediate experience, whereas avijjā more often marks fundamental not-knowing in broader doctrinal sequences. Moha should also be heard as one of the unwholesome roots and as the factor that keeps greed and aversion from being seen clearly.

context_rules.context: In most ethical and doctrinal contexts
In explanatory prose emphasizing muddle rather than outright false view
context_rules.notes: Acceptable alternate when it reads more naturally.
Use sparingly because it is more literary.

context_rules.context: When the passage is naming one of the unwholesome roots
context_rules.notes: Keep the analytical root framework visible.

example_phrases.translation: delusion is a root of the unwholesome

example_phrases.notes: Useful contrastive expression in practice contexts.
